content_of_post_to_comment:
Like a lot of people here trying to build better daily discipline, my work requires me to learn constantly. As a developer, I open YouTube multiple times a day to look up short technical walkthroughs, quick framework fixes, or specific tutorials.

The problem was that YouTube isn't built like an encyclopedia—it's built like a casino.

I’d open the site intending to watch a 4-minute video on a specific coding bug, but the homepage recommendation grid would immediately grab my eyes. Before I even typed in the search bar, I’d see three interesting video essays or news breakdowns. I’d tell myself, "I'll just watch this one quick 10-minute clip while I drink my coffee."

Two hours later, my focus state was completely shattered, I hadn't solved the bug, and I felt guilty for wasting the morning.

A few weeks ago, I decided to treat YouTube as a pure search utility instead of an entertainment platform during my work hours. I realized that my willpower wasn't the issue—the visual cues on the screen were.

Here is the exact framework I used to break the loop:

Strip the Homepage: I forced my setup to hide the entire homepage recommendation grid. When I open the site, the screen is completely blank except for the top search bar. If I don't have a specific topic to search for, there is literally nothing to look at.

Lock the Related Sidebar: The sidebar on the right side of a video is designed to keep you watching "just one more." Blocking those recommendations completely stopped me from clicking onto a second video after finishing the tutorial I actually needed.

Remove the Comment Section: Reading comments feels like low-effort engagement, but it drags your mind out of deep work. Hiding them keeps my brain focused purely on the video's information.

The "Search and Exit" Rule: I only open the platform when I have a precise question written down. I search it, watch the video, and close the tab immediately.

Since making this shift, my daily screen time on video platforms dropped significantly, but more importantly, my deep-work blocks stopped getting interrupted.

If you find yourself constantly losing hours to "productive procrastination" or educational rabbit holes, try removing the visual recommendations entirely and forcing yourself to rely strictly on intentional searches.

How do you guys handle necessary tools that double as distraction traps?
comment: The "search and exit" rule is probably the part I'll steal from this. I've noticed even blocking recommendations doesn't help if I open YouTube without a clear question, because my brain starts looking for something interesting to consume. Turning the platform into a tool with a specific purpose changes the habit from browsing to solving.
